---
title: "First data analysis with R in a Quarto document"
---

This quick tutorial will show you how to use Quarto documents within Positron. We will:

- Install Positron
- Create an R project
- Install the packages we need for our analysis
- Open a Quarto document and
- Explore some data


## Install Positron

Positron is a free and source available code editor for data scientists. To install Positron on your computer, visit [positron.posit.co](http://positron.posit.co) and click the "Download" button.

![]("images/download-button.png")

Follow the instructions to install Positron.

## Make a Folder for Your Analysis

It's good practice to give each analysis its own folder. A folder keeps your scripts, data, and outputs together in one tidy place. Positron makes it easy to create one.

Here's how:

1. Open Positron
2. Go to New > New Folder from Template...

!["New Folder from Template option in the File menu"](images/new-folder-from-template.png)

3. Pick **R Project** from the template list

!["R Project template option in the New Folder from Template dialog"](images/python-project-template.png)

4. Select a location for your project folder and give it a name. Then click Next.

!["Create Python Project dialog"](images/create-python-project.png)

On the next screen, select a version of R to use from those available on your computer. Then click Create. Positron will scaffold the folder for you and launch an R console session automatically.

!["Create R Environment dialog"](images/create-r-environment.png)

## Install packages

Now that we have a project set up, we need to install the packages we will use for our analysis. For this tutorial, we will use the `tidyverse` package, which includes `dplyr` for data manipulation and `ggplot2` for visualization.

::: note

`tidyverse` is a collection of R packages designed for data science. It includes packages that share a common design philosophy and cover the basic data science tasks: data import, tidying, transformation, visualization, and more.

:::

To install `tidyverse`, navigate to the R Console in Positron (View > Console) and run:

```r
install.packages("tidyverse")
```

::: tip
You only need to install a package once. After that, you load it with `library()` each time you start a new R session.
:::

## Open a Quarto document

Now that we have our project set up and the packages we need installed, we can open a Quarto document to start our analysis.

To create a new Quarto document in Positron:

1. Click New > New File in the New menu
2. Choose Quarto Document from the list of file types

This will create a new `.qmd` file in your project folder and open it in the source editor. You can now start writing text and code.

::: tip
A Quarto document is a plain text file that mixes markdown text with code cells. It is similar to an R Markdown document, but with more features and support for multiple languages. Learn more about Quarto at <https://quarto.org>.
:::

A Quarto document begins with a YAML header enclosed in `---` marks. This header contains metadata like the document's title and output format. Below the header, you write markdown text and insert code cells to run R code.

To insert a new R code cell, use the keyboard shortcut `Ctrl+Shift+I` (`Cmd+Shift+I` on Mac). This inserts a fenced code block that looks like this:

````
```{r}

```
````

You can type your R code inside the cell and click the Run button (▶) at the top right of the cell to execute it. The output will appear directly below the cell in the editor.

## Explore some data

Now that we have our Quarto document open, we can start exploring some data. For this tutorial, we will use the `starwars` dataset, which is a built-in dataset in the `dplyr` package. It contains information about Star Wars characters, including their name, height, mass, species, and more.

Insert a new code cell with `Ctrl+Shift+I` and add the following code, then click the Run button:

```r
library(tidyverse)
```

This loads the `tidyverse` packages into your R session. Next, insert another code cell and run:

```r
starwars <- dplyr::starwars
```

Notice that the Variables pane to the right of the editor now shows the `starwars` dataset. You can use the Variables pane to explore the columns in the dataset. Or you can click the grid-like icon to open the dataset in the Positron Data Explorer.

Let's narrow the dataset down to human characters, using the code generated by the Data Explorer:

```r
starwars_humans <- starwars |>
  filter(species == "Human")
```

Next we will visualize the relationship between height and mass for human Star Wars characters using `ggplot2`:

```r
ggplot(starwars_humans, aes(x = height, y = mass)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = gender), size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Star Wars Humans: Height vs Mass",
    x = "Height (cm)",
    y = "Mass (kg)"
  )
```

And finally, a quick summary table showing average height and mass by gender:

```r
starwars_humans |>
  group_by(gender) |>
  summarize(
    avg_height = mean(height, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_mass = mean(mass, na.rm = TRUE),
    n = n()
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(avg_height))
```

## Render and preview your document

A major benefit of Quarto is that you can render your document into a polished report with a single shortcut. Press `Ctrl+Shift+K` (`Cmd+Shift+K` on Mac)—or click the **Preview** button (▶) at the top of the editor. 

Positron will render your `.qmd` file into an HTML document and display a live preview in the Viewer pane alongside your source code. Every time you render, Quarto will execute all of the code cells in order and produce a complete report with your text, code, and outputs.

::: tip
Turn on **Render on Save** (in Settings or by adding `editor: render-on-save: true` to the YAML header) and your report will refresh every time you save the file.
:::

## Save your document

To save your document, click the Save button in the toolbar or use the keyboard shortcut `Ctrl+S` (`Cmd+S` on Mac). The `.qmd` file will appear in the File Explorer on the left.

::: tip
If your project is version-controlled with Git, you can commit and push straight from the Git pane in Positron. This is handy for sharing your analysis or keeping a backup on GitHub.

Learn more about Git in Positron at <https://positron.posit.co/git.html>
:::

## Conclusion

That's it! You've set up an R project in Positron, installed packages, and built a small data analysis inside a Quarto document.

To keep going, explore the [Positron Guides](https://positron.posit.co/welcome.html) and the [Quarto documentation](https://quarto.org).
